title: Pappa al pomodoro
subtitle: Tuscan bread and tomato soup
excerpt: Stale Tuscan bread dissolved into a thick tomato soup with garlic, basil, and abundant olive oil. It should be dense enough to stand a spoon in, not a thin broth with croutons. Eat warm in autumn or at room temperature in summer, both are traditional.
sourceNotes: Pappa al pomodoro is documented as a Florentine and broadly Tuscan preparation from the late nineteenth century. Pellegrino Artusi includes it in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain) as a simple workman's dish, noting that the name pappa refers to mushy or porridge-like food. The dish became celebrated in the twentieth century through both its presence in Florentine trattoria culture and its appearance in a popular children's song (1958, Italian television). It is an archetype of cucina povera.

content.text: Pappa al pomodoro is one of the simplest dishes in Italian cooking and one of the most misunderstood. It is not soup with pieces of bread in it, and it is not a thin broth that bread has been soaked in. It is a thick, porridge-like mass where bread and tomato have become inseparable, with neither texture distinct from the other. If you can see individual pieces of bread, it has not cooked long enough.

Use stale, crusty bread: sourdough, ciabatta, or any open-crumbed white bread that has dried out. The bread should be at least a day old and ideally two. Fresh bread makes the dish pasty and flavourless. The tomato can be tinned or fresh and very ripe; in summer, use the ripest tomatoes you can find. In all other months, use good tinned plum tomatoes.

items.name: Tinned tomatoes
items.prepNote: whole plum, crushed by hand; or 1 kg very ripe fresh tomatoes, roughly chopped

items.name: Ciabatta
items.prepNote: day-old or stale; torn into rough chunks

items.name: Basil
items.prepNote: leaves picked; stalks used in cooking, leaves to finish

items.name: Extra-virgin olive oil
items.prepNote: good quality; 4 tbsp for cooking, 2 tbsp to finish

content.text: Warm 4 tablespoons of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a heavy pot over medium heat. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the basil stalks. Cook for 2 minutes until the garlic is pale gold and fragrant. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes, crushing them with a spoon as they go in. Season with salt. Simmer for 10 minutes.

Add the torn {{ciabatta}} bread and the {{stock-vegetable}} of stock. Stir well to submerge all the bread in the tomato liquid. Bring to a gentle simmer. Cook for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring every few minutes and pressing the bread with the back of the spoon, until the bread has completely dissolved into the tomato and the soup is thick, almost solid. It should hold its shape briefly when you drag a spoon through it.

Remove the basil stalks. Tear in the fresh {{basil}} leaves and stir through. Taste for salt and pepper. Let the pappa sit for 10 minutes before serving. It thickens further as it rests. Serve in bowls with the remaining {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} drizzled generously over. Eat warm or at room temperature; both are traditional.

items.fix: Cook for the full 15 to 20 minutes, stirring and pressing; if still thin, cook uncovered for 5 more minutes; the bread should be entirely dissolved
items.cause: Too much liquid, or bread not dissolved long enough
items.symptom: Pappa is thin and soupy rather than thick

items.fix: Use bread that is at least a day old and has dried out; fresh bread releases starch that makes the dish gluey
items.cause: Fresh bread used instead of stale
items.symptom: Bread taste is floury and pasty

items.fix: Cook the tomato base for the full 10 minutes before adding bread; use whole plum tomatoes, not passata
items.cause: Tomatoes not cooked long enough, or low-quality tinned tomatoes used
items.symptom: Tomato flavour is sharp and acidic

items.fix: Use a generous drizzle of good extra-virgin olive oil at the end; pappa al pomodoro needs a lot of olive oil to taste right
items.cause: Finishing olive oil skipped or insufficient; the olive oil is essential, not garnish
items.symptom: Dish tastes flat despite good tomatoes

content.text: Pappa al pomodoro started in Florence and spread across Tuscany. The 19th-century cookery writer Artusi set it down as a workman's lunch. Stale bread got a second life in summer tomatoes and olive oil. The dish went national in the late 1950s after a children's TV show featured a song about it. A peasant dish turned into a comfort-food classic. In Florence it still sits on the most traditional trattoria menus next to ribollita and trippa alla fiorentina. It is part of the Florentine cucina povera, now served with pride because it was once thought too humble for a menu.
